items.question: Write the tanh formula and its relationship to sigmoid.
Why is tanh called zero-centered and why does that matter?
items.answer: Tanh is odd with f(0)=0, so symmetric inputs produce activations centered at zero. Sigmoid outputs are always positive (center 0.5), which can bias weight updates in the next layer. Zero-centering often speeds convergence in hidden and recurrent layers.

items.question: What is the derivative of tanh and its maximum value?
items.answer: d/dx tanh(x) = 1 − tanh²(x). Maximum is 1 at x = 0. Compare sigmoid max derivative 0.25. Gradient → 0 when |x| is large (saturation).

items.question: When would you use tanh instead of ReLU or sigmoid?
items.answer: Tanh for bounded, zero-centered hidden/recurrent states (RNN h_t, LSTM candidate cell). Sigmoid for (0,1) probabilities and gates. ReLU for deep feedforward hidden layers to avoid saturation.

items.question: How does tanh saturation cause vanishing gradients?
items.answer: When |x| is large, tanh(x) ≈ ±1 so 1 − tanh²(x) ≈ 0. Backprop multiplies many small factors through saturated layers, shrinking gradients to early layers — same class of problem as deep sigmoid nets, motivating ReLU for depth.
